speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- this is reel five with Alex Reifer. Reel five. At the end of reel four, you were telling me about this green photo holder, I guess. We don't want to call it an album because of what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo holder consists of pictures which I took it from a person, from a captain, from the 16th Panzer Division SS, which he was in Auschwitz a certain time and he took himself these pictures. Because nobody else can take pictures like that in a concentration camp, how these pictures are made. It's impossible. The officers from the concentration camp wouldn't allow a person should come in, an outsider, to take this type of pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- how did you discover this, these photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, when we take somebody, [POLISH] we're calling it, a [POLISH]. They bring him to me. If I-- if I didn't catch him myself, my people catch him to bring him right away to me, to bring him to me. I talk to him nice to start with, you know. And I speak to him in German, which I speak perfect German. And I ask him the name and I write everything down. I don't write, but I have a person that's writing for me on the side, you know, because I only look on his face and I know normally what-- any-- any-- any type of-- which he don't want to say the truth, I know. I feel it. When I-- when you look on a person which is against you fighting and you look on his eyes and the face and if he don't say it to you, you-- you-- you feel it. And I interview him, I talk to him. And then after that-- after I interview him completely, everything is finished-- I look at him, I took away his watch, I took off the ring, I took off his shoes, and I took everything which he has in his pocket. He has got two watches, one hand watch, I remember that, and a-- and a pocket watch, a gold pocket watch. The gold pocket watch I think also is Jewish, you know. And then-- then I-- I find this one, I find this-- this-- this-- this-- this particular like it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you opened it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going away, start crying. What can I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I shoot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But did you talk to him about the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I-- when I opened it up. I came back from crying cuz it's not nice to cry between people which you organize. It's not-- you couldn't do it. I go away, cry, finish, I wrap up, come back. And I tell him that. I tell him, I'm Jewish. Why you kill so many Jews? He said, I didn't kill, I took pictures. He start talking to me already which he said different before. But he-- he told me all the truth completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're-- just for the-- for the people who are watching, we will be filming these photographs, most of which are extremely graphic, and we will be filming them at the end of the interview. So when we do film them, we just want everyone to know that they're very difficult photographs and there are also some photographs of some experiments, particular on a woman. So I think that whoever watches this tape, catalogers or anyone in the future, please understand that these are very difficult photos. Did you-- did you shoot him right after these photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He asked me food several times, he asked me water several. I give him nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't know his name to this day.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it written down put in. Not only his name, I have written down a lot of names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- But you don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. If-- if you want to put that down now. I think it's OK. So did you-- what did you do with that? Did you put it in your pocket and hold onto it? What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I put it on the table which, I was [INAUDIBLE] table. Later on, I took it with me, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you carry it with you all through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So as the Russian-- as the as the Russian Army was moving west, as the Soviets were moving west, what-- can you describe what you were doing and-- I mean, you've just given us, I think, a very big overview of what you did during the war. But how long did it take for you as-- as you were liberating Poland? Why don't you tell me as Poland was being liberated what you were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland liberated is not-- it was not so important for me at all, even though I was born in Poland. But the main thing is to finish the war. This was important for me. And in the time when we was moving fast and we came to a certain part in the direction from Berlin, I was in charge of several hundred people. And then we already got into Berlin and we was working on the Reichstag, we were attacking the Reichstag. And it was several days till we took the Reichstag. And when I got in the Reichstag, I cut my finger with a knife and I write up in Jewish my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the Reichstag?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Reichstag, inside. [SIGH] [DRINKS] Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And this was May 4. Of 19--? May 4, was it 1945? Then we transferred from Berlin to Grabov. The [POLISH], not far from Hamburg. And then I was the first officer which I met in America 82nd Army. And I was very friendly with him. And the first-- the first man which I find-- which I find was the name is Stanislav [POLISH]. He was a staff sergeant in the 82nd. And he has pictures from it. You have pictures from him. Also I met officers. I met some officers. And later on, I was introduced to high officers, to the main-- I was introduced to high officers. But normally, I was all dealing mostly with people. When I was in charge of that city, Grabov-- it's a big city. And they make me the mayor from the city-- I mean, the commandant from the city. I organized special kitchens because we transferred the people which want to-- which lived in the America side in Belgium or France or anyplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people which live in this side, they bring it in and I organized kitchens. I cleaned up two streets completely for these people. And soldiers were standing on both sides not to let in anybody else, only the people which-- which is transferring, you see? Let me-- let me take you back, though. First of all, did you participate in the battle for Budapest at all, or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing to do with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. As you were moving west from Russia, from the Soviet Union, into Germany through Poland-- actually, when you got to the Ukraine-- maybe that's a better way to-- through the Ukraine into Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not far from Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Did you ever see any mass graves where the Jews had-- you know, where the Ukrainians and the Germans had shot Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I personally didn't see it. But it was-- they told me that it was plenty graves. You see, I was a very busy man. I was working mostly day and night, mostly nights. This I can tell-- mostly nights I was working. Days I slept a couple hours, three or four hours, five hours, you know, because and I've got vodka. You know, if not I couldn't sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we was-- our-- our organization, the reconnaissance was-- the feeding the food was excellent. The best food what exist, we got. And I was going normally every couple of days to [POLISH], Army and [POLISH] Division. And down there in the [POLISH] Army Division also by Jewish people. And if I tell them I need 10 liter-- you know what liter means, right? Leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: A liter is a half a gram.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a liter.

speaker: interviewee
text: A liter, yeah. 10 liter vodka he give me. And the reason was also they have got-- they ask me, why you give me? Why-- why-- what does Alex give me? You see, what we did is this way. When we lost people, when we lose people-- you see I lost in a week one or every two weeks three people-- dead, you know, lost. The uniforms I bring over to this guy, I give it to him. And he sells it someplace and this I got vodka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, as you were moving west, did you come across any of the camps like Treblinka? Did you see where Sobibor had been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- Auschwitz and Treblinka, I was only when I got a vacation after-- when I was on vacation in Poland I was in Auschwitz myself. I came home in uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's after the war, but I was in the Army till 1946. I was close to a year longer as a-- as a-- as the war end. The war end May 9. I ended-- I [LAUGHS] ended the war May 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess my question was, as you were moving west with the Reconnaissance Division, where-- how you moved across Europe, did you move through the places, through-- through the killing-- through the death camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't-- we didn't move into any city. Our operation was not to go in any kind of city, in any kind of building, only to go on the road whenever we need to go straight. Because we must be in a certain point in at a certain time. Also, we protect the Army when the Army was moving. We protect them. And it's not an easy job. It's a big job.

speaker: interviewer
text: You indicated that the food for your division was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about--

speaker: interviewee
text: We got our own kitchen. We, the Reconnaissance Group, got our own kitchen. If I need-- if I need to serve, I tell them I want to have chicken and they don't have chicken, right, I sent four people to bring chicken, to bring live chicken. They kill them and [INAUDIBLE]. We have got the best food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the rest of the conditions like-- the sleeping conditions, the sanitary conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sleeping conditions on front-- this is normal for every division, for every soldier-- we sleep and-- and-- and we build a bunker from the top that the sharpeners shouldn't move in. And-- and-- and we built from-- from the-- from the air-- how do you call from the-- from the base. And that's all. We-- we lay down and sleep. You don't have any any kind of mattresses, you don't have any kind of cover up. Your cover-- I sleeped even till today is a couple of days already after the war, till today I sleep with a hand like that-- ask my wife-- hand like that because this is the way I slept in the front. I-- I-- I rest my hand-- my-- my head on my hand. That's all. And we sleep-- we-- we never get undressed when we sleep. No front soldier has a right to take off anything at all. The gun, [LAUGHS] the gun is in the front here. Yeah. And who is taking off a thing, he is not a professional. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in Berlin, what were-- [SIGHS] what was it like? What did the Soviet Army do to the German people when you arrived? Well, first of all, describe for me the Battle of Berlin, if you can at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Battle of Berlin was very tough. It was a very hard battle, unbelievable. It's impossible even to describe. It was-- it was with planes from the Russian side. We didn't see any German planes because there was already more-- more destroyed [INAUDIBLE]. But we've got a very, very big bubble with the-- with the SS, which was like-- we calling it-- later on, we didn't call them anymore SS, we called them the Dead Army. But he-- he was afraid if he goes in, that we take him, he said he has got a grenade here. [? he blowed himself up ?], he was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean any officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans, yeah. Officers and soldiers. Mostly it was Hitler's people from the-- from the Hitler Jungend we calling it. And the Hitler Jungend is very tough because that what they tell him that is he doing. He doesn't think in his mind himself, only what the officer told him to do. This is the way he is doing it. And this was a tough job.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, at the very end of the war basically there were young boys who were in the army now who had been drafted. Were you-- what were you encountering in terms of age? Was it those young ones that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one are you talking, the Russian Army or the--

speaker: interviewer
text: German.

speaker: interviewee
text: German Army was young people. I-- I find people from 16 years old already in the German Army. He bring in-- he bring in anybody he can to-- to stop the Russian Army. And he couldn't do, it was impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the actual battle was raging-- the fighting, the actual day-to-day combat, you know the guns, the machine guns, the grenades-- were used there participating in that, or were you waiting for Berlin to be taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Are you talking about Berlin?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was participating in it. Because the battle normally was-- mostly was with tanks and artillery. And our artillery was-- was three times so much artillery as the German have got in this particular part of Berlin, you see. And we-- we was bombarding all around the Reichstag day and night, 24 hours a day. We was-- we was in Berlin. We was doing a very big job because we they bring in-- they bring in the toughest division from the Russian Army from the second-- the second front. We was from the second front, we was. The second front and then they came in from the first front and came in. And it was-- it was big. Unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, if you talk to somebody like I talk to you, you couldn't listen. You must say, eh? So much bombardment it was. What about the Soviet soldiers, what about the Russian soldiers? Did you witness anything they did to German women, to-- what did they do against the Germans themselves? Did you see anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did, but we was-- we was against it also. It was a lot of rapes on the German-- from the Russian Army to the Germans. We-- we was against it, but-- and we didn't like it. We took them off from things and we send them to the back. We didn't put them in jail or whatever it is because we don't have jails down there. And it was a lot of problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- so you did you handle it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We interview him and we send him back. If he is a sergeant, we lower him to a soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- what about pillaging, I guess, or taking-- taking things from Germans, from what was left of Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taking-- taking things from German was no-- was no-- against it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the Reichstag blew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was-- the Reichstag didn't blow up.

speaker: interviewer
text: It didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Reichstag still stays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I'm sorry, when they blew the top off where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was there. I was there on the side. But I was not in the Reichstag at this time. But the Reichstag stays.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know where the-- where the Nazi emblem--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there when that was blown up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I was there, but I don't remember. [SIGHS] Mostly for officers when we took Berlin, mostly were drunk. Not drunk. I mean, you know, instead of 200 grams vodka a day, they give us 500.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you participate in any liberation of any camps at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's you that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was the date that you actually wrote in blood on the Reichstag wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the 4th, before the transfers to-- to-- to Grabov. I got a big cut on the finger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do in Grabov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grabov? When I came to Grabov, I met the American Army, the 82nd Division, and they make me the commandant from the City of Grabov, like a mayor. And I organized the people which coming into this particular side. I just tell you I cleaned up two-- two streets completely. I threw out all the Germans. They couldn't do nothing [INAUDIBLE] at all. I threw them out and I put in the people from the camps. And I organized the kitchen, soldier, put in soldiers in the kitchen. And the soldiers was cooking all day and all night. I confiscate-- from the Germans, I confiscate the bakeries. And they must-- I check before I confiscate. They must finish the flour which they have in stock, they must bake. And we took all the bread. We didn't give anybody else. I confiscate all kinds of food which is in the warehouses in Berlin and [POLISH], from [INAUDIBLE]. I was commandant from [POLISH]. [POLISH] is like Queens or something, you know a part of-- of Berlin. And I give the people to eat, everything. I've got so many cooks, I've got over 20 cooks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jews coming back through Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I met-- I met a lot of Jews, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked to them in Yiddish, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find out from those Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Nothing ?] They was in different camps and [INAUDIBLE] in this camp and that camp, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the true dimensions of what had actually happened during those six years-- did you-- did you understand the true dimension of the holocaust? Did you understand the whole time what was going on, or once that liberation occurred did you really find out much more than you ever knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find out a lot of things. I find out-- I knew-- I knew about Auschwitz. I knew about Birkenau. I knew about Dachau. I knew about all the mostly concentration camps. I knew. But when I talked to the people which are were there, I still don't believe it. Unbelievable. Unbelievable that-- that they can-- a country which is in the [INAUDIBLE] in the [INAUDIBLE] name German intelligent people could do something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Grabov, did you-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Grabov. I was working in Grabov a long time. And I meet-- oh, I've got meetings with the American Army and all of them was writing to my uncle in New York. And-- and after I asked-- I asked the-- the commandant from-- from the-- a minute. And this is in Berlin, a different way. OK. After Grabov, when I was there, they-- in charge of all the stuff was [POLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH] was in charge. I asked [POLISH] to release me from the army. And he couldn't release me from the army. He said, you know what, Alex? My wife is coming from Moscow and as soon as she comes, I will transfer you to-- to Moscow. I said, OK. His wife came, we took her out from airport, and he give me and I flew into-- to Moscow, to the general stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Moscow, they give me a vacation. I asked for a vacation, four weeks. They give me vacation. And I was flying. And I could go-- in my uniform with my medals which I got, I can go any part, nobody ask me [INAUDIBLE], nothing, nobody. I don't care. He-- he was afraid to come to me. And I came to Auschwitz and I visit the concentration camp, I visit-- I want to find out my family. And I find-- they told me that a person which I knew before, the name was [POLISH], a girl, I was going to school with her brother, to the Jewish school. And she is in-- in-- in Sosnowiec, in a different city. She is still alive. And I was going to Sosnowiec because they told me also that all the Jews from Auschwitz which were alive stay in Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Sosnowiec, I got the address, I visit her. She was very happy to see me. And then she told me she need a favor from me. I said, OK, [POLISH], anything. I said, what do you need? She said, listen, I have a boyfriend-- down there they don't call-- here they call them boyfriend, down there they call him she will marry him, OK? And they put him in jail. I said, they put him in jail? What-- what he did to put him in jail? He was in concentration camp. She said, yeah. He was dealing with-- with German coins, gold coins and they catch him and they put him in jail. I said, write up the name of the person. And she write up the name of the person. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was thinking. And I said, OK, [POLISH], we go. Where is he in jail? She said, he is in [POLISH]. [POLISH] is a different city. I was going with her to [POLISH]. And every city in Poland in this particular time and the commandant was Russian. I came in to the-- to the main office and I said to the [POLISH] down on the table, I said-- my name in Russian is [POLISH] is my name. And I told him I want to see the colonel, the commandant. He said-- he want to say something, but when I took off my overcoat and he saw the metals, he didn't say nothing. He stand up and he was going in and he said, yeah, the [POLISH] will see you-- will see you right away. And I got in-- I got in, [POLISH] stand up. He said-- he said to me, you are [POLISH]. I said, what-- what-- what you came in here? I said, listen, I came from the front and I-- came and I want to see my family and I find out that one of my cousins is in jail. And I give him the name. And he said, do you know what you catch him? I said, yeah. They told me that some coins he has got. I say, he has got coins from-- from his parents from before. And he called in [POLISH] and you tell him, take Mr. Reifer-- I mean, you know, he didn't say mister-- and go take out the man from jail. He was going to me, I took him out. What was his name, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. He is still live here in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to mention his name-- Germans, you know. He's a big shot. He is very big. I will tell you the name privately, I don't want to tell you on--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I know who it is. I'm pretty sure I know who it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you-- I can't set the camera, but I think I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? two minutes ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did you actually discover-- how did you actually find out about your parents and your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I find out that they are not here anymore, the perished in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're gonna change--

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed the whole family in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the reel.
